metadata.summary: GUI test tool and automation framework that uses Accessibility (a11y) technologies to communicate with desktop applications.

Ed Rousseau \<<mailto:rousseau@redhat.com>>,
David Malcolm \<<mailto:dmalcolm@redhat.com>>,
Vitezslav Humpa \<<mailto:vhumpa@redhat.com>>,
Michal Odehnal \<<mailto:modehnal@redhat.com>>
Author-email: Zack Cerza \<<mailto:zcerza@redhat.com>>, Ed Rousseau \<<mailto:rousseau@redhat.com>>, David Malcolm \<<mailto:dmalcolm@redhat.com>>, Vitezslav Humpa \<<mailto:vhumpa@redhat.com>>, Michal Odehnal \<<mailto:modehnal@redhat.com>>
Project-URL: Homepage, <https://gitlab.com/dogtail/dogtail>
Requires-Python: >=3.6
Description-Content-Type: text/x-rst
License-File: COPYING

# dogtail

dogtail is a GUI test tool and UI automation framework written in Python. It uses Accessibility (a11y) technologies to communicate with desktop applications. dogtail scripts are written in Python and executed like any other Python program.

Dogtail works great in combination with behave and qecore (based on behave and dogtail), especially if you're interested in using it with modern Wayland-based GNOME. Please see this article for more details on how we mainly use it:
[Automation through accessibility](https://fedoramagazine.org/automation-through-accessibility/).

Other than that, dogtail should work with any desktop environment that still runs atspi with Xorg.

# News

## dogtail 1.0

After more than five years of continuous experimentation and development, we are excited to finally release Dogtail 1.0—a Wayland-enabled version of Dogtail! How did we achieve this? It was made possible by the [gnome-ponytail-daemon](https://gitlab.gnome.org/ofourdan/gnome-ponytail-daemon), originally crafted by Olivier Fourdan. This tool allows us to perform actions in a Wayland GNOME session in a similar way to how we have been doing so with X functions.

Dogtail 1.0.4 - Minor release again containing two updates! / Dec 4 2024
\- sniff: Fixes in About dialog and getting rid of GObject deprecations MR #43
\- tree: Moving all matches of "push button" to check also "button" - Fixes issue #35

dogtail 1.0.3 - Minor release again containing two updates / Nov 13 2024
\- utils: get_current_x_window_position: Support running without a wm
\- tree : Improve center property calculation in Node class
Thanks Samuel Thibault and mikigo for the MR and discussion!

dogtail 1.0.2 - Cummulated two updates - bugfixes - and releasing as minor release. / Oct 24 2024
Update for sniff: Switched using XPM icons to PNG as the most recent gdk pixbug no longer supports them
(fixes no icons in most recent system) - thanks to Jiri Prajzner
Using specific configuration for the debug logger to prevent duplicit logging - MR#39 - thanks to Jiri Kortus

dogtail 1.0.1 - Rebased with a number of contributions that were done to the master branch while we were / Sep 6 2024
working on Wayland and we have accidentaly not included them in 1.0.
See <https://gitlab.com/dogtail/dogtail/-/issues/31>

Also returned couple of modules we thought were of no use (tc, wrapped) but were proved otherwise to keep 1.0 fully compatible with 0.x.

## How does it work in brief?

The core functionality relies on the Screen Cast and Remote Desktop API, enabling us to "connect" to either a specific window or the entire screen to simulate user input actions like key presses, typing, and—most importantly—mouse actions on specific coordinates. Ponytail uses the window list from `org.gnome.Shell.Introspect` to identify windows. Dogtail then ensures the correct window is connected via the Remote Desktop API, allowing input actions to be accurately executed within it.

On the AT-SPI tree and accessibility's "introspection" side, not much has changed—input has primarily been the challenge on Wayland. The main difference is that only "local" window coordinates are known for UI items. To address this, we always connect to the specific window we're working with, and Ponytail's `connectWindow` ensures these local coordinates are translated to global ones internally.

## What does this mean for users?

Dogtail handles all of this logic seamlessly, so in practical use, the user doesn't need to worry about coordinate types or window connections. In fact, the vast majority of our tests work identically in both X and Wayland sessions using the Dogtail API. When running on an X session, Dogtail will use the traditional X functions to handle input and operate as it always has.

## A long journey to 1.0

We began working on the Wayland-enabled version over five years ago (originally available in the `devel/wayland` branch). It has taken all this time to ensure the solution is robust and reliable enough to warrant a 1.0 release. This version includes all the Wayland-related developments and other changes (like the GTK4 support tweaks mentioned below) and has been extensively tested. Importantly, it is backward compatible with the entire Dogtail API that has been available so far. This release includes all modules from the 0.9.x series, most notably the "procedural" module, which we plan to deprecate in favor of a completely "tree-based" approach in Dogtail 2.0.

That release will also involve significant cleanup, major code refactoring, and a transition from `pyatspi` to directly introspected `atspi`. It will also contain updated code examples and Dogtail's unittests should pass with GitLab pipelines re-enabled.

For more details on these upcoming changes, see issue #29.

## 1.0+ Important: Handling GTK4 Application Windows in Dogtail

For GTK4 applications, disabling window shadows is essential to ensure accurate positioning by Dogtail. With shadows disabled, we encounter a consistent coordinate offset, which we've preconfigured as `dogtail.config.gtk4Offset`. In the case of working with fullscreen windows, the offset is 0, and we manage to detect that on-the-fly both in X11 and Wayland sessions. However, this process requires `python-xlib`, even for Wayland sessions, leveraging Xwayland to ascertain resolution information, as no more direct method we've found is currently available for Wayland.

When window shadows are active, the perceived offset can vary significantly, influenced by factors such as the specific application, window size, and scaling settings. To ensure consistent behavior across applications, disabling shadows is recommended.

Disabling Shadows in GTK4:

To disable window shadows, add the following CSS to your GTK4 configuration (`~/.config/gtk-4.0/gtk.css`):

```css
window, .popover, .tooltip {
    box-shadow: none;
}
```

# Installation

Dogtail is available with PIP! (1.0 inclusion pending). If you'd like to use it with Wayland GNOME, you also need to get the `dogtail-ponytail-daemon`: <https://gitlab.gnome.org/ofourdan/gnome-ponytail-daemon>. We do not have that as a dependency in PIP as it compiles C code.

Check your distro for packages! If not at the latest version, we also have released tarballs for download: <https://gitlab.com/dogtail/dogtail/tree/released>

For details see the INSTALL file.

# Dependencies

Python bindings for your distribution, e.g. python-apt or rpm-python

PyGObject and GNOME-Python

Applications to test, e.g. from the GNOME desktop: <http://gnome.org/>

# Using

Currently, GNOME and GTK+ applications are supported, in both Xorg and Wayland sessions.
See examples for direct dogtail use or check the following article for more information:
[Automation through accessibility](https://fedoramagazine.org/automation-through-accessibility/).

If you are using KDE instead, set the environment variable `QT_LINUX_ACCESSIBILITY_ALWAYS_ON=1` when launching the respective program.
You can add this line to your profile file:

```bash
export QT_LINUX_ACCESSIBILITY_ALWAYS_ON=1
```

Depending on the version, `QT_ACCESSIBILITY=1` may be needed instead.

For very old KDE/QT versions (approximately 4.8.3 to 5.0), you have to install the `qt-at-spi` QT plugin and set the environment variable `QT_ACCESSIBILITY` to 1.

First, enable accessibility support in your GNOME session with the following command:

```bash
gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.interface toolkit-accessibility true
```

This only affects newly-started applications, so you may want to log out and log back in again.

Should you run `sniff` first, or be using `dogtail-run-headless-next` or `qecore-headless` scripts to handle your sessions, the accessibility will be auto-enabled for you.

# Bugs

Please report any bugs at:

: <https://gitlab.com/dogtail/dogtail/issues>

We have deprecated our mailing lists as well as the IRC channel. Please use our GITLAB for issues and merge requests! (Or possibly <https://github.com/vhumpa/dogtail> for your pull requests should you prefer to use GitHub, but gitlab.com is preferred)

classes.name: dogtail.procedural.FocusError
documentation: WARNING: Here There Be Dragons (TM)                                        #
If you don't understand how to use this API, you almost certainly don't    #
want to read the code first. We make use of some very non-intuitive        #
features of Python in order to make the API very simplistic. Therefore,    #
you should probably only read this code if you're already familiar with    #
some of Python's advanced features. You have been warned.                  #

modules.name: dogtail.utils
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: dogtail.utils.screenshot
documentation:
This function wraps the ImageMagick import command to take a screenshot.

The file argument may be specified as 'foo', 'foo.png', or using any other
extension that ImageMagick supports. PNG is the default.

By default, screenshot filenames are in the format of foo_YYYYMMDD-hhmmss.png .
The timeStamp argument may be set to False to name the file foo.png.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.run
documentation:
Runs an application.
[For simple command execution such as 'rm *', use os.popen() or os.system()]
If dumb is omitted or is False, polls at interval seconds until the application is finished
starting, or until timeout is reached. If dumb is True, returns when timeout is reached.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.doDelay
documentation:
Utility function to insert a delay (with logging and a configurable
default delay)

functions.name: dogtail.utils.isA11yEnabled
documentation: Checks if accessibility is enabled via DConf.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.bailBecauseA11yIsDisabled
documentation: Accessibility is detected as enabled. End the execution if there are no exceptions.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.enableA11y
documentation: Enables accessibility via DConf.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.checkForA11y
documentation: Checks if accessibility is enabled, and halts execution if it is not.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.checkForA11yInteractively
documentation:
Checks if accessibility is enabled, and presents a dialog prompting the
user if it should be enabled if it is not already, then halts execution.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.waitForWindow
documentation:
Wait for window to appear. Currently wayland only.
name can be a window 'title' as reported via Node.name
or an app id (from .desktop file. i.e. "org.gnome.Calculator.desktop")
Returns true on success, false on x11.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.get_current_x_window_position
documentation:
This is a helper to get window possition (top left corner) solely by means of
Xlib (direct X calls) - without a11y. This is targeted to be used with !GTK4!
apps only - which don't support giving GLOBAL coords of their nodes under Xorg
(as well as on wayland). By getting the win location and adding that up together
with local coords, we can make actions with GTK4 apps under Xorg as well.
Not to be mixed with what we do with local coords and local functions on W.

Imports are used locally here not to bring uncessery deps in most cases - because
this is for rather a corner case... GTK4 apps will mostly be run in wayland and
with Xorg most likely much much less.

functions.name: dogtail.utils.get_screen_resolution
documentation:
Additional helper function with similar reasoning as the get_current_x_window_position needed
to work with GTK4 apps - even on wayland! (needs Xwayland running for xlib to get this info).
We need this to detect whether the frame is in fullscreen mode in order to either use the config.gtk4Offset
or not. There is no offset when window is full-screen. Se README for more info on this and shadows.

classes.name: dogtail.utils.Highlight

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Highlight.area_draw
documentation: Draw a rectangle on the screen.

Hightlight class used by Blinker. Display a red rectangle corresponding to the Accessibility
node position and size via Gtk.Window class.

classes.name: dogtail.utils.Blinker

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Blinker.destroyHighlight
documentation: Remove the highlight.

Blinker class used to highlight the Accessibility node in graphical interface.
This highlight will be seen for 1 second before it will disappear.

classes.name: dogtail.utils.Lock

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.__init__
documentation:
You can change the default lockdir location or name. Setting randomize to
False will result in no random string being appened to the lockdir name.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.__exit_unlock
documentation: Removes the lock upon exiting headless.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.lock
documentation:
Creates a lockdir based on the settings on Lock() instance creation.
Raises OSError exception of the lock is already present. Should be
atomic on POSIX compliant systems.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.unlock
documentation:
Removes a lock. Will raise OSError exception if the lock was not present.
Should be atomic on POSIX compliant systems.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.locked
documentation: Check if locked directory exists.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.Lock.__getPostfix
documentation: Generate a length of 5 random string that serves as file name suffix.

A known sytem-wide mutex implementation that uses atomicity of the mkdir operation in UNIX-like
systems. This should be used mainly to provide mutual exclution in handling possible collisions
among multiple script instances. You can choose to make randomized single-script wise locks
or a more general locks if you do not choose to randomize the lockdir name. Set unLockOnExit
to True to enable automatic unlock when scipt process exits to avoid having to unlock manually.

classes.name: dogtail.utils.GnomeShell

methods.name: dogtail.utils.GnomeShell.getApplicationMenuList
documentation:
Returns list of all menu item nodes. Searches for the menu by a reference item.
Provide a different item name, if the 'Quit' is not present - but beware picking one
present elsewhere, like 'Lock' or 'Power Off' present under the user menu.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.GnomeShell.getApplicationMenuButton
documentation: Returns the application menu 'button' node as present on the gnome-shell top panel.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.GnomeShell.getApplicationMenuItem
documentation:
Returns a particilar menu item node. Uses a different 'Quit' or custom item name for
reference, but also attempts to use the given item if the general reference fails.

methods.name: dogtail.utils.GnomeShell.clickApplicationMenuItem
documentation:
Executes the given menu item through opening the menu first followed by a click at the
particular item. The menu search reference 'Quit' may be customized. Also attempts to
use the given item for reference if search fails with the default/custom one.

Utility class to help working with certain atributes of gnome-shell.
Currently that means handling the Application menu available for apps
on the top gnome-shell panel. Searching for the menu and its items is
somewhat tricky due to fuzzy a11y tree of gnome-shell, mainly since the
actual menu is not present as child to the menu-spawning button. Also,
the menus get constructed/destroyed on the fly with application focus
changes. Thus current application name as displayed plus a reference
known menu item (with 'Quit' as default) are required by these methods.

modules.name: dogtail
documentation: -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

modules.name: dogtail.rawinput
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.ponytail_check_is_xwayland
documentation: Detect Xwayland windows in order to use globals and recordMonitor

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.click
documentation: Synthesize a mouse button click at (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.point
documentation: Synthesize a button point at (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.doubleClick
documentation: Synthesize a mouse button double-click at (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.press
documentation: Synthesize a mouse button press at (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.release
documentation: Synthesize a mouse button release at (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.absoluteMotion
documentation: Synthesize mouse absolute motion to (x,y)

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.absoluteMotionWithTrajectory
documentation:
Synthetize mouse absolute motion with trajectory. The 'trajectory' means that the whole motion
is divided into several atomic movements which are synthetized separately.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.relativeMotion
documentation:
Synthetize a relative motion from actual position.
Note: Does not check if the end coordinates are positive.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.drag
documentation: Synthesize a mouse press, drag, and release on the screen.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.dragNodeToNode
documentation:
Drag source_node onto dest_node. These are tree.Node objects. Takes positions
of these Nodes directly, so you don't have to calculate end enter them directly.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.dragWithTrajectoryGlobal
documentation:
Synthetize a mouse press, drag (including move events), and release on the screen
For use on Wayland - as this function forces using global coords, although we get
local ones from a11y. So this function is targeted to be used for inter-window drags
on Wayland, where you will need to questimate the coords. Which is doable i.e by having
source window placed on the left *half* of the screen (local coords will be very similar
to globals perhaps with the top panel offset).

Having 'trajectory' appears to be necessary on Wayland for any drags. Use 'dragWithTrajectory'
or just 'drag' on X sessions like in pre-wayland version of dogtail.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.dragWithTrajectory
documentation:
Synthetize a mouse press, drag (including move events), and release on the screen
Please note, that on Wayland, this function works for drags only within a single window.
On X this function works with global coords and equals dragWithTrajectoryGlobal

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.typeText
documentation:
Types the specified string, one character at a time.
Please note, you may have to set a higher typing delay,
if your machine misses/switches the characters typed.
Needed sometimes on slow setups/VMs typing non-ASCII utf8 chars.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.uniCharToKeySym
documentation: Use GDK to get the key symbol for a given unicode character.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.keyNameToKeySym
documentation: Use GDK to get the key symbol for a key name.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.keyNameToKeyCode
documentation:
Use GDK to get the keycode for a given keystring.

Note that the keycode returned by this function is often incorrect when
the requested keystring is obtained by holding down the Shift key.

Generally you should use uniCharToKeySym() and should only need this
function for nonprintable keys anyway.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.pressKey
documentation:
Presses (and releases) the key specified by keyName.
keyName is the English name of the key as seen on the keyboard. Ex: 'enter'
Names are looked up in Gdk.KEY_ If they are not found there, they are
looked up by uniCharToKeySym().

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.keyCombo
documentation:
Generates the appropriate keyboard events to simulate a user pressing the
specified key combination.

comboString is the representation of the key combo to be generated.
e.g. '<Control><Alt>p' or '<Control><Shift>PageUp' or '<Control>q'

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.holdKey
documentation: Press and hold the key specified by keyName.

functions.name: dogtail.rawinput.releaseKey
documentation: Releases the holded key specified by keyName.

documentation:
Used by the recording system.

Generate the Python source code that will carry out this search.

methods.name: dogtail.path.SearchPath.getRelativePath
documentation:
Given another SearchPath instance, if the other is 'below' this
one, return a SearchPath that describes how to reach it relative
to this one (a copy of the second part of the list).    Otherwise
return None.

methods.name: dogtail.path.SearchPath.getPrefix
documentation: Get the first n components of this instance as a new instance.

methods.name: dogtail.path.SearchPath.getPredicate
documentation: Get the predicate.

Class used by the recording framework (and for more verbose script
logging) for identifying nodes in a persistent way, independent of the
style of script being written.

Implemented as a list of (predicate, isRecursive) pairs, giving the
'best' way to find the Accessible wrapped by a Node, starting at the
root and applying each search in turn.

This is somewhat analagous to an absolute path in a filesystem, except
that some of searches may be recursive, rather than just searching
direct children.

modules.name: dogtail.tc
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: dogtail.tc.TC

methods.name: dogtail.tc.TC.compare
documentation:
Compares 2 strings to see if they are the same. The user may specify
the encoding to which the two strings are to be normalized for the
comparison. Default encoding is the default system encoding.
Normalization to extended 8 bit charactersets is not supported.

When the origin of either baseline or undertest is a text file whose
encoding is something other than ASCII, it is necessary to use
codecs.open() instead of open(), so the file's encoding may be
specified.
String comparison function
The Test Case Superclass

classes.name: dogtail.tc.TCString
documentation: String Test Case Class
String Test Case subclass

classes.name: dogtail.tc.TCNumber

methods.name: dogtail.tc.TCNumber.compare
documentation:
Compares 2 numbers to see if they are the same. The user may specify
how to normalize mixed type comparisons via the type argument.
Compare 2 numbers by the type provided in the type arg
Number Comparaison Test Case Class

methods.name: dogtail.tc.TCBool.compare
documentation:
If _bool is True, pass.
If _bool is False, fail.

methods.name: dogtail.tc.TCNode.compare
documentation:
If baseline is None, simply check that undertest is a Node.
If baseline is a Node, check that it is equal to undertest.

modules.name: dogtail.config
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: dogtail.config._Config

methods.name: dogtail.config._Config.__createDir
documentation:
Creates a directory (if it doesn't currently exist), creating any
parent directories it needs.

If perms is None, create with python's default permissions.

methods.name: dogtail.config._Config.load
documentation: Loads values from dict, preserving any options already set that are not overridden.

methods.name: dogtail.config._Config.reset
documentation: Resets all settings to their defaults.

Contains configuration parameters for the dogtail run.

scratchDir(str):
Directory where things like screenshots are stored.

dataDir(str):
Directory where related data files are located.

logDir(str):
Directory where dogtail.tc.TC*-generated logs are stored.

scriptName(str) [Read-Only]:
The name of the script being run.

encoding(str)
The encoding for text, used by dogtail.tc.TCString .

actionDelay(float):
The delay after an action is executed.

typingDelay(float):
The delay after a character is typed on the keyboard.

runInterval(float):
The interval at which dogtail.utils.run() and dogtail.procedural.run()
check to see if the application has started up.

runTimeout(int):
The timeout after which dogtail.utils.run() and dogtail.procedural.run()
give up on looking for the newly-started application.

searchBackoffDuration (float):
Time in seconds for which to delay when a search fails.

searchWarningThreshold (int):
Number of retries before logging the individual attempts at a search.

searchCutoffCount (int):
Number of times to retry when a search fails.

searchShowingOnly (boolean):
Whether to only search among nodes that are currently being shown.

defaultDelay (float):
Default time in seconds to sleep when delaying.

childrenLimit (int):
When there are a very large number of children of a node, only return
this many, starting with the first.

debugSearching (boolean):
Whether to write info on search backoff and retry to the debug log.

debugSleep (boolean):
Whether to log whenever we sleep to the debug log.

debugSearchPaths (boolean):
Whether we should write out debug info when running the SearchPath
routines.

absoluteNodePaths (boolean):
Whether we should identify nodes in the logs with long 'abcolute paths', or
merely with a short 'relative path'.

ensureSensitivity (boolean):
Should we check that ui nodes are sensitive (not 'greyed out') before
performing actions on them? If this is True (the default) it will raise
an exception if this happens. Can set to False as a workaround for apps
and toolkits that don't report sensitivity properly.

debugTranslation (boolean):
Whether we should write out debug information from the translation/i18n
subsystem.

blinkOnActions (boolean):
Whether we should blink a rectangle around a Node when an action is
performed on it.

fatalErrors (boolean):
Whether errors encountered in dogtail.procedural should be considered
fatal. If True, exceptions will be raised. If False, warnings will be
passed to the debug logger.

checkForA11y (boolean):
Whether to check if accessibility is enabled. If not, just assume it is
(default True).

logDebugToFile (boolean):
Whether to write debug output to a log file.

logDebugToStdOut (boolean):
Whether to print log output to console or not (default True).

modules.name: dogtail.i18n
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: dogtail.i18n.translate
documentation:
Look up srcString in the various translation databases (if any), returning

Look up the named package and find all gettext mo files within it and its

Singleton list of TranslationDb instances, to be initialized by the script with

classes.name: dogtail.i18n.TranslationDb

methods.name: dogtail.i18n.TranslationDb.getTranslationsOf
documentation:
Pure virtual method to look up the translation of a string.
Returns a list of candidate strings (the translation), empty if not found.

Note that a source string can map to multiple translated strings. For
example, in the French translation of Evolution, the string "Forward" can
translate to both
(i) "Faire suivre" for forwarding an email, and
(ii) "Suivant" for the next page in a wizard.
Abstract base class representing a database of translations

classes.name: dogtail.i18n.GettextTranslationDb
documentation:
Implementation of TranslationDb which leverages gettext, using a single
translation mo-file.

Compare the test string against either the translation of the original
string (or simply the original string, if no translation was found).

classes.name: dogtail.distro._SolarisPackageDb
documentation: getVersion not implemented because on Solaris multiple modules are installed
in single packages, so it is hard to tell what version number of a specific
module.

classes.name: dogtail.distro.Distro
documentation:
Class representing a distribution.

Scripts may want to do arbitrary logic based on whichever distro is in use
(e.g. handling differences in names of packages, distribution-specific
patches, etc.)

We can either create methods in the Distro class to handle these, or we
can use constructs like isinstance(distro, Ubuntu) to handle this. We can
even create hierarchies of distro subclasses to handle this kind of thing
(could get messy fast though)

modules.name: dogtail.tree
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

variables.name: dogtail.tree.gotWnck
documentation: pragma: no cover

classes.name: dogtail.tree.SearchError
documentation: The widget was not found.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.NotSensitiveError
documentation: The widget is not sensitive.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.ActionNotSupported
documentation: The widget does not support the requested action.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Action

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Action.do
documentation: Performs the given tree.Action, with appropriate delays and logging.
Class representing an action that can be performed on a specific node.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Node

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.__setupUserData
documentation: Setup user data dictionary.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.debugName
documentation: Debug name assigned during search operations.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.debugName
documentation: Debug name setter.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.dead
documentation: Is the node dead (defunct)?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.children
documentation: A list of this Accessible's children.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.window_id
documentation: Return window id of a node.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.window_has_focus
documentation: Check if window is focused.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.doActionNamed
documentation:
Perform the action with the specified name. For a list of actions
supported by this instance, check the 'actions' property.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.actions
documentation:
A dictionary of supported action names as keys, with Action objects as
values. Common action names include:
    'click' 'press' 'release' 'activate' 'jump'
    'check' 'dock' 'undock' 'open' 'menu'

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.combovalue
documentation: The value (as a string) currently selected in the combo box.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.URI
documentation: The value of user data linkAnchor.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.text
documentation:
For instances with an AccessibleText interface, the text as a
string. This is read-only, unless the instance also has an
AccessibleEditableText interface. In this case, you can write values
to the attribute. This will get logged in the debug log, and a delay
will be added.

If this instance corresponds to a password entry, use the passwordText
property instead.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.caretOffset
documentation: For instances with an AccessibleText interface, the caret offset as an integer.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.caretOffset
documentation: Caret offset setter.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.position
documentation: A tuple containing the position of the Accessible: (x, y)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.size
documentation: A tuple containing the size of the Accessible: (w, h)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.extents
documentation: A tuple containing the location and size of the Accessible: (x, y, w, h)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.center
documentation: A tuple containing the center position of the Accessible:(x, y)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.contains
documentation: Can tell if and x and y coordinates are inside a desktop coordinates.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.getChildAtPoint
documentation: Get a child in coordinates x, y.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.grabFocus
documentation:
Attempts to set the keyboard focus to this Accessible.
Affected by rhbz 1656447 on Wayland! We do different actions based on type of the node
to get it focused there as workaround. For some we can do nothing (push button)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.click
documentation:
Generates a raw mouse click event, using the specified button.
    - 1 is left,
    - 2 is middle,
    - 3 is right.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.doubleClick
documentation: Generates a raw mouse double-click event, using the specified button.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.point
documentation: Move mouse cursor to the center of the widget.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.labeler
documentation:
'labeller' (read-only list of Node instances):
The node(s) that is/are a label for this node. Generated from 'relations'.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.labelee
documentation:
'labellee' (read-only list of Node instances):
The node(s) that this node is a label for. Generated from 'relations'.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.sensitive
documentation: Is the Accessible sensitive (i.e. not greyed out)?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.showing
documentation: Is the Accessible showing (rendered and visible) on the screen?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.focusable
documentation: Is the Accessible capable of having keyboard focus?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.focused
documentation: Does the Accessible have keyboard focus?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.checked
documentation: Is the Accessible a checked checkbox?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.isChecked
documentation: Is the Accessible a checked checkbox? Compatibility property, same as Node.checked.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.visible
documentation:
Is the Accessible set to be visible? A widget with set attribute
'visible' is supposed to be shown and doesn't need to be actually
rendered. On the other hand, a widget with unset attribute 'visible'

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.selectAll
documentation: Selects all children.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.deselectAll
documentation: Deselects all selected children.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.select
documentation: Selects the Accessible.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.deselect
documentation: Deselects the Accessible.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.isSelected
documentation: Is the Accessible selected? Compatibility property, same as Node.selected.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.selected
documentation: Is the Accessible selected?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.selectedChildren
documentation: Returns a list of children that are selected.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.value
documentation: The value contained by the AccessibleValue interface.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.value
documentation: Setter for the value contained by the AccessibleValue interface.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.minValue
documentation: The minimum value of Node.value

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.minValueIncrement
documentation: The minimum value increment of the Node.value

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.maxValue
documentation: The maximum value of self.value

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.typeText
documentation: Type the given text into the node, with appropriate delays and logging.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.keyCombo
documentation: Press keys in combination.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.getLogString
documentation:
Get a string describing this node for the logs,
respecting the config.absoluteNodePaths boolean.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.satisfies
documentation: Does this node satisfy the given predicate?

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.dump
documentation: Dumping a tree structure of the node.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.getAbsoluteSearchPath
documentation:
Generate a SearchPath instance giving the 'best'
way to find the Accessible wrapped by this node again, starting
at the root and applying each search in turn.

This is somewhat analagous to an absolute path in a filesystem,
except that some of searches may be recursive, rather than just
searching direct children.

Used by the recording framework for identifying nodes in a
persistent way, independent of the style of script being
written.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.getRelativeSearch
documentation:
Get a (ancestorNode, predicate, isRecursive) triple that
identifies the best way to find this Node uniquely.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.__nodeIsIdentifiable
documentation: Checks if given node can be identified by labellee, name or parent.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node._fastFindChild
documentation: Searches for an Accessible using methods from pyatspi.utils

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.findChild
documentation:
Search for a node satisyfing the predicate, returning a Node.

If retry is True (the default), it makes multiple attempts, backing off and retrying
on failure, and eventually raises a descriptive exception if the search fails.

If retry is False, it gives up after one attempt.

If requireResult is True (the default), an exception is raised after all
attempts have failed. If it is false, the function simply returns None.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.findChildren
documentation:
Find all children/descendents satisfying the predicate. You can also use lambdas in
place of the pred that will enable search also against pure dogtail Node properties
(like showing). I.e: "lambda x: x.roleName == 'menu item' and x.showing is True".
isLambda does not have to be set, it's kept only for api compatibility.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.findAncestor
documentation:
Search up the ancestry of this node, returning the first Node
satisfying the predicate, or None.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.child
documentation:
Finds a child satisying the given criteria.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.
Various wrapper/helper search methods:

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.isChild
documentation:
Determines whether a child satisying the given criteria exists.

This is implemented using findChild, but will not automatically retry
if no such child is found. To make the function retry multiple times set retry to True.
Returns a boolean value depending on whether the child was eventually found. Similar to
'child', yet it catches SearchError exception to provide for False results, will raise
any other exceptions. It also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.menu
documentation:
Search below this node for a menu with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.menuItem
documentation:
Search below this node for a menu item with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.textentry
documentation:
Search below this node for a text entry with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.button
documentation:
Search below this node for a button with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.childLabelled
documentation:
Search below this node for a child labelled with the given text.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.childNamed
documentation:
Search below this node for a child with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.tab
documentation:
Search below this node for a tab with the given name.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.getUserVisibleStrings
documentation:
Get all user-visible strings in this node and its descendents.

(Could be implemented as an attribute)

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Node.blink
documentation: Blink will hightlight the current node on the screen. Using Blinker from utility.

A node in the tree of UI elements. This class is mixed in with
Accessibility.Accessible to both make it easier to use and to add
additional functionality. It also has a debugName which is set up
automatically when doing searches.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.LinkAnchor
documentation:
Class storing info about an anchor within an Accessibility.Hyperlink, which
is in turn stored within an Accessibility.Hypertext.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Root

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Root.applications
documentation: Get all applications.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Root.application
documentation:
Gets an application by name, returning an Application instance
or raising an exception.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.
Root class used to get data from Accessible.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Application

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Application.dialog
documentation:
Search below this node for a dialog with the given name,
returning a Window instance.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Application.window
documentation:
Search below this node for a window with the given name,
returning a Window instance.

This is implemented using findChild, and hence will automatically retry
if no such child is found, and will eventually raise an exception. It
also logs the search.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Application.getWnckApplication
documentation:
Get the wnck.Application instance for this application, or None

Currently implemented via a hack: requires the app to have a
window, and looks up the application of that window

wnck.Application can give you the pid, the icon, etc
Application class used to get data from Accessible.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Window

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Window.getWnckWindow
documentation: Get the wnck.Window instance for this window, or None

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Window.activate
documentation: Activates the wnck.Window associated with this Window.
Window class used to get data from Accessible.

classes.name: dogtail.tree.Wizard

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Wizard.currentPage
documentation:
Get the current page of this wizard.
This is currently a hack, supporting only GnomeDruid

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Wizard.getPageTitle
documentation:
Get the string title of the current page of this wizard
This is currently a total hack, supporting only GnomeDruid

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Wizard.clickForward
documentation:
Click on the 'Forward' button to advance to next page of wizard.
It will log the title of the new page that is reached.
This will only work if your libgnomeui has accessible buttons see above.

methods.name: dogtail.tree.Wizard.clickApply
documentation:
Click on the 'Apply' button to advance to next page of wizard.
What if it's Finish rather than Apply?
This will only work if your libgnomeui has accessible buttons see above.

Note that the buttons of a GnomeDruid were not accessible until recent versions of libgnomeui.
This is http://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=157936 and is fixed in
gnome-2.10 and gnome-2.12 (in CVS libgnomeui) there's a patch attached to that bug.
This bug is known to affect FC3; fixed in FC5
